name: Teddy Charles
abstract: Teddy Charles, born Theodore Charles Cohen (April 13, 1928 – April 16, 2012) was an American jazz musician and composer, whose instruments were the vibraphone, piano, and drums.

concerts.label: Horizons in Jazz featuring Dizzy Gillespie, George Shearing, and Others
Midnight Performance: Contemporary Jazz Composers Series
Kool Jazz Festival: Musicians for Monk
Kool Jazz Festival: A Night of Genius-Artists Celebrate Mingus

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: working with Teddy was very beneficial to me... We worked most of the time with a quartet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: it was J.R Montrose, Teddy Charles and Mingus and myself. So that’s that quartet... Well that’s when Teddy—we both worked with Teddy Charles.
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Crow
about_quotes.evidence: Teddy Charles I think was my first really jazz group. I worked with his trio and he taught me all the Bebop changes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Crow interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: One of the memorable concerts there was one that was headed up by Teddy Charles, but included both Booker Ervin and Booker Little, both of whom had far too short careers, both of whom I did get to know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Most
about_quotes.evidence: he asked Teddy Charles, Teddy Cohen, he changed his name, to write this string quartet album. So I had a string quartet album at that time I guess in the 50s... I played clarinet and flute on it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sam Most interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Gibbs
about_quotes.evidence: I think maybe popularity or something, because Milt Jackson and I were the only two vibes players on the scene at the time who got well known as vibes players until Teddy Charles and Joe Roland
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Gibbs interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, November 5, 2021 [interview 2 of 2]
